model.vision: A platform that produces predictably-engineered software and delivers and operates it on the customer's behalf.

objectives.statement: Predictable delivery: customers receive a duration, cost, and risk estimate before any code is written, and the delivered system arrives within the bounds of the chosen option.

Method conformance: every project that ships through the platform is the output of a complete pass through business alignment, requirements scrubbing, volatility-based decomposition, project design with options, and reviewed construction — no skipped phases.

Revenue only from operation: the platform earns a usage-based fee solely for operating a delivered system on the customer's behalf; it never charges for design, construction, or the customer's own compute — the only billable quantity is what a running operated system consumes.

Customer self-sufficiency, platform dependency: the customer walks through The Method themselves but does not need engineering staff — the platform's AI Workers play every technical Method role (architect, senior, junior, test, ops) while the customer remains the architect-of-record and requirements source.

Cost transparency at decision time: whenever the customer is asked to commit money, the projected operating cost is presented as a traceable, per-option figure, so every financial decision is informed by a forecast of what running the system will cost.

Extensibility to new categories of work: the platform can absorb new kinds of work products and new kinds of automated workers without re-architecting; the Method itself remains stable as the infrastructure beneath them changes.
Audit and intervention: a human can take over, replay, or override any in-flight project; nothing the platform does is opaque or unrecoverable.

Customer-driven automation: the customer can drive the entire platform through their own AI agent and their own automation — not only through a human interface — with the same capabilities exposed to software and people alike.

model.mission: Design and build a collection of software components that together (a) carry a customer through The Method as a guided workflow, (b) orchestrate, dispatch, and supervise the automated work that produces the system, (c) deploy and operate the delivered system on the customer's behalf, and (d) bill the customer a usage-based fee for operating that system. The mission is to orchestrate, not to author code; specific build experiences and operational offerings are assembled from these components.

items.definition: The party that owns one or more projects and pays aiarch for hosting when aiarch operates the delivered system; construction itself runs on the customer's own accounts at no charge from aiarch.
The customer-side person who drives a project through The Method as architect-of-record — typically not an engineer — approving every gated decision the platform's Workers draft.
A platform staff member with authority to intervene in a running project or operated system.
Any entity — an AI agent by default, or a human contractor — that produces work products on behalf of a project.
A person who uses the customer's deployed application; they pay the customer, never aiarch, and never interact with the platform.
A customer's undertaking to design, build, and optionally operate one software system, progressing through the system-design, project-design, and construction phases.
The durable record of a project's current phase, its Method artifacts, and each artifact's review status.
A single typed Method work product belonging to a project — mission, glossary, architecture, plan, and so on — carrying a review status of awaiting, committed, rejected, or withdrawn.
A description of one business activity the customer's system must support, with its actors, trigger, and flow of steps.
A use case that captures the essence of the customer's business rather than a permutation of another; the small set the architecture must be validated against.
The computed duration, cost, and risk of one project option.
One candidate way to schedule and staff a project — such as normal, compressed, or subcritical — presented with its estimate for the customer's decision.
The software the platform built and now runs on the customer's behalf, consuming hosting capacity that aiarch meters and bills.
The mirror of a single project activity in the customer's work-item tracker, opened when planning completes, updated during construction, and closed when the work is accepted.
The customer's choice of how far the platform's responsibility extends — building and delivering the system only, or also hosting and operating it.
The hosting and compute consumption an operated system accrues, metered as the sole basis of the customer's bill.
The customer's declared expectation of end-user load on the delivered application, used during project design to forecast operating cost.
The bounded time window over which a customer's metered usage is accumulated into a single service invoice.
The amount a customer owes aiarch for one billing period's metered hosting usage, charged to their stored payment instrument.
The first project phase, in which the platform leads the Architect User from business vision to a validated, volatility-based architecture.
The second project phase, in which the platform turns the architecture into staffing-and-schedule options with estimated duration, cost, and risk.
The third project phase, in which Workers build, review, and integrate the system component by component against the chosen project option.
The ongoing phase after delivery, available by scenario choice, in which the platform hosts, scales, observes, and bills for the customer's system.
Capturing or refining a single Method artifact together with the Architect User, who approves or rejects it at a review gate.
Forecasting what a system will cost to operate as a function of usage — at decision time from declared assumptions, and while running from observed load.
The decision point closing project design, where the customer chooses among the presented project options.
Assigning a discrete construction activity to a Worker and supervising it to completion or failure.
Evaluating a produced change against the project's design before it is accepted into the project.
Measuring construction progress against the plan at a regular cadence and triggering replanning when progress deviates significantly.
Placing a built system onto managed runtime capacity, or releasing that capacity when the system is withdrawn.
The platform's own decision to raise, lower, pause, or resume an operated system's capacity in response to observed demand, within the customer's chosen policy.
The autoscaling decision to hold an inactive operated system at zero capacity — and zero compute cost — while keeping it reachable for the next request.
Converting an operated system's observed consumption into billable usage attributed to its customer.
Pricing a customer's metered usage for a billing period and charging the resulting service invoice to their stored payment instrument.
Pausing, replaying, overriding, or taking over an in-flight project or operated system when it is stuck, unhealthy, or failing to pay.
The single per-project store holding everything the platform persists for that project — its state, its Method artifacts, and its constructed outputs.
The customer's own code-hosting system, where the project repository lives and where construction work runs on the customer's account and credentials.
The customer's external project-management system in which the project's activities are mirrored as work items.
The substrate that durably sequences and persists the platform's own long-running workflows — phase drives, billing cycles, and operation loops — across restarts and failures.
The external payment system through which aiarch charges customers' stored instruments for its service invoices.

items.statement: The platform must carry a customer through the production of a system without that customer assembling tools themselves.
The architect user must be able to co-author each Method artifact with an automated assistant.
The platform must enforce phase ordering and gate progression on artifact validity.
The platform must produce, for a planned project, multiple project options (normal, decompressed, compressed, subcritical), each with duration, cost, and risk.
Cost estimation must be sensitive to which workers will perform which activities.
Which kind of worker fulfills each Method role (architect, senior, junior) must be allowed to vary.
The platform must accept architect-user interaction over more than one entry channel.
The platform must deploy and operate delivered systems on the customer's behalf.
The platform must produce, for a completed project, everything needed to deploy and operate the delivered system without manual handoff.
The platform must serve heterogeneous and growing customer types without baking assumptions about customer size, internal team, or sophistication into its design.

The platform bills the customer usage-based for hosting of operated systems only — no flat subscription and no charge for construction or automated-assistant work — and never invoices for, or shares in, end-user revenue.
All state and source the platform produces for a customer must reside in one customer-owned store per scenario, owned outright and retrievable by the customer at any time.
The platform orchestrates and observes construction while build, test, and deployment execute on compute the customer owns.
The platform must support pause, replay, override, and human takeover of any in-flight construction activity.
The platform must automatically adjust an Operated System's capacity (scale up, scale down, idle-pause, resume-to-baseline) from observed telemetry, per a customer-tunable policy.

The platform must expose, for any Operated System, the current cost run-rate, projected monthly cost, and a what-if scale curve, so the Operator and the Customer can see how operating cost responds to load.
Project estimation at SDP-review time must include a usage-to-operating-cost curve and a sensitivity band for ± uncertainty around the customer's declared usage assumptions, per project option.
The platform must support an evolving, curated set of infrastructure building blocks for deployed customer systems, such that adding or retiring a building block is absorbed without re-architecting.

items.name: System Design Phase Workflow
items.rationale: The sequence of Phase 1 activities (business alignment → requirements analysis → volatility identification → core use cases → architecture → operational concepts → standard check) will evolve as architectural taste matures: new sub-skills added (security review, accessibility review), existing ones reordered, gating rules changed, artifact schemas refined. A customer completing a project today and starting another in 18 months will encounter an evolved Phase 1 step set, and the platform must absorb that evolution without being rebuilt. Evolves independently of Phase 2.

items.name: Project Design Phase Workflow
items.rationale: The sequence of Phase 2 activities (planning assumptions → activity list → network → normal/decompressed/subcritical/compressed → risk modeling → SDP review) will evolve as project-planning sophistication grows: cone-of-uncertainty intervals replacing 5-day quanta, new option types, a new operational-risk curve. Evolves independently of Phase 1 — improvements to the SDP review do not in themselves change requirements scrubbing. Lives on axis 1 because the platform evolves it for everyone at once.

items.name: Construction Phase Workflow
items.rationale: The sequence of Phase 3 activities (hand-off model → per-component contract design → construction → weekly tracking → scope-change handling) will evolve as construction practice matures: continuous earned-value replacing weekly tracking, automatic contract-drafting at hand-off, AI-driven scope-change proposals. Evolves independently of Phase 1 and Phase 2.

items.name: Operation Phase Workflow
items.rationale: The choreography by which the platform operates a delivered system — the deploy-after-construction ceremony, reconcile cadence, the health→intervention→autoscale sequencing, delinquency application, withdraw — will evolve as operating practice matures: event-driven reconciliation replacing polled sweeps, progressive-delivery gates (canary, automatic rollback) in the deploy step, self-healing sequences before escalation. Same shape as the three Method-phase workflow volatilities: what varies is the Manager's activity sequence, NOT the policies it consults (Intervention Policy, Autoscaling Policy) nor the runtime it drives (Operated Runtime) nor where it records state (Operational State Store). Evolves independently of the Method phases and of the billing cycle.

items.name: Billing Workflow
items.rationale: The choreography of a billing cycle — read cycle usage, compute the charge, execute the charge, record the outcome, unpaid-charge sweeps, dunning, delinquency escalation — will evolve: dunning sequences, proration on mid-cycle plan changes, pre-authorization holds before large charges. Deliberately distinct from Service Pricing (the pricing policy BillingEngine pivots on — what the charge IS) and from Payment Provider (which gateway executes the money movement): what varies here is the cycle's activity sequence itself.

items.name: Construction Estimation Model
items.rationale: How construction duration, cost, and risk are computed will change as workers change, as historical data accumulates, and as the team refines the model. Construction estimation only — the operating-side forecast is split out as Operation Estimation Model. The two evolve on independent timelines (construction estimation matures with worker/build telemetry; operation estimation matures with fleet/usage telemetry).

items.name: Operation Estimation Model
items.rationale: How operating cost is forecasted as a function of usage. Used at design-time (SDP review: usage-to-operating-cost curve + payout-vs-shortfall forecast) and at op-time (OperationsManager: current run-rate, projected monthly cost, what-if curve over observed usage). Evolves as telemetry deepens and as Customer App Infrastructure expands. Independent of Construction Estimation Model because the inputs differ (usage curves + infrastructure cost model vs. build graph + worker throughput) and the outputs serve different decisions.

items.name: Customer App Infrastructure
items.rationale: The list of technologies that a customer's deployed app is permitted to use. Launch set: Go + Temporal + Postgres + Git + S3. The list evolves as the platform broadens (e.g., adding pgvector for AI-feature apps, WASM compute units). The fact of constraint is design given; the contents of the constrained set is what is volatile. Encapsulated by Strategy registrations on AutoscalerEngine and OperationEstimationEngine — a cross-cutting axis with no single owning component. Every customer operates on the same permitted set at any given moment — the set broadens for everyone at once.

items.name: Service Pricing
items.rationale: How aiarch prices its own service to the user evolves system-wide over time. Current instance (2026-06-14): a usage-based hosting fee for operated systems only (DEPLOYED — OPERATED); no flat subscription; construction is not charged (runs on the user's own Claude Code token and CI minutes). Tomorrow: per-seat tiers, free-tier subsidies, hosting-resource-class pricing, volume discounts. This is open-ended, will ripple, and must not be a hardcoded constant — it is the policy billingEngine pivots on as a Strategy. Lives on axis 1 because aiarch re-prices its service for everyone as its cost structure and market position change.

items.name: Intervention Policy
items.rationale: What counts as 'stuck', who is notified, when a human is paged, and what authority they have to override is policy that will change as the platform matures. Trigger surface includes construction variance, unhealthy operated state, and billing failures (a user's stored instrument declines an aiarch service invoice). Early on, every variance escalates to a single operator. Later, severity tiers, customer SLA classes, and self-healing rules emerge.

items.name: Review Policy
items.rationale: Who must review a produced change, from what perspective, and against which reference artifact is policy that will evolve as the platform's engineering practice matures. Today: a code change is reviewed by the owners of each inbound/outbound neighbour component, plus the change's own component owner. Tomorrow: new reviewer roles, adjacency rules keyed on layer or risk, deeper review for higher-risk components, bidirectional amend rules. Lives on axis 1 because the platform evolves the policy system-wide as practice matures. Phase-3 construction only — Phase-1 role choreography is the Manager's fixed sequence.

items.name: Autoscaling Policy
items.rationale: At the same moment, different operated apps run different capacity policies: SLA-class tiers, per-app idle-pause thresholds, scale-from-zero cold-start tolerance, predictive vs. reactive scaling, operator overrides. What counts as 'this app's capacity is right' for one customer's app is wrong for another's. The rule set also matures over time, but the dominant axis is per-app/per-customer policy variance at one moment. Symmetrical with Intervention Policy in shape but structurally distinct: Intervention Policy answers 'is this app broken and what do we do?'; Autoscaling Policy answers 'is this app's capacity right and what do we do?' Two axes, two encapsulators.

items.name: Customer Interaction Channel
items.rationale: Different customers reach the platform through different entry channels at the same moment. Today: a browser hitting a JSON HTTP API, and an MCP client (remote MCP over HTTP). Tomorrow: IDE plugins, Slack apps, agentic clients, voice. The kind of caller is what varies here. Encapsulated by the Client tier — each Client component (WebClient, MCPClient) owns one channel's specifics so Managers never see channel. As of 2026-06-14 the customer-facing MCP instance is remote MCP over HTTP only (the MCP-over-stdio instance dropped from the customer surface with the LOCAL story).

items.name: Customer Interaction Transport
items.rationale: Even for a fixed channel, the wire-level delivery mechanism will evolve: JSON-over-HTTP/1.1 today, server-sent-events or WebSockets for streaming draft co-authoring tomorrow; MCP-over-HTTP today, MCP-over-streamable-HTTP tomorrow. Independent of channel: a channel can keep its identity while its transport changes. Each Client component owns sole ownership of its transport stack — including the SchedulerClient, whose varying trigger-delivery mechanism (durable-runtime schedule callback today; cron or event triggers later) is this same axis. Managers never see transport types.

items.name: Durable Execution Runtime
items.rationale: Which durable-execution runtime sequences Manager workflow code. Current instances: Temporal in the cloud deployment; an in-process embedded executor in the test profile; other durable executors later. The same Manager code runs unchanged against either — the executeWorkflow/signal/query/timer/schedule/spawn verbs are stable while the runtime behind them varies per deployment profile at one moment and migrates over time. Encapsulated by durableExecutionAccess. Narrowed from the former two-faced definition: the agentic-coding pipeline face is now its own axis, Construction Pipeline Target, because the two vary independently with different change drivers.

items.name: Construction Pipeline Target
items.rationale: Which CI/agentic-pipeline system executes dispatched construction jobs. Current instance: the user's own GitHub Actions running claude-code-action; instance set: claude-code-action → GitHub Copilot coding agent → GitLab CI; the dry-run/test profile is a stubbed instance of the pipeline target behind the same verbs. At one moment different customers run different pipeline targets — it is THEIR CI — and a customer can migrate CI without any Manager change. The submit/observe/cancel verbs are stable; the pipeline system behind them is what varies. Encapsulated by constructionPipelineAccess. Split out of the former Durable Execution Runtime: the pipeline target varies per customer with their CI choice, while the durable Manager-sequencing executor varies per deployment profile and platform migration — two independent axes, two encapsulators.

items.name: Operated Runtime
items.rationale: Same shape as Durable Execution Runtime but for the runtime that hosts delivered systems in the DEPLOYED — OPERATED scenario. Today: GitOps over Kubernetes via Argo CD + Argo Rollouts on the aiarch hosting cluster. Three faces: convergence infrastructure (how desired-state-in-the-repo becomes actual-state-in-the-cluster), compute cost attribution (per-customer cost-of-goods surface), and runtime-status transport (how the platform learns about state changes in the operated runtime). All three encapsulated behind OperatedRuntimeAccess.

items.name: Construction Hand-Off Policy
items.rationale: At any given moment, different customers have chosen different splits of human vs. automated work along the Method's architect / senior / junior hand-off. Some customers want every line of code reviewed by a senior human; others want fully automated construction with the customer acting only as the architect.

items.name: Source Control Target
items.rationale: At the same moment, different scenarios put the per-project repo — and everything aiarch does through it — on different source-control providers/targets. Current instances: the user's own GitHub (via a GitHub App, cloud) and an ephemeral throwaway git repo (test); GitLab later. The axis covers both faces of the provider, which co-vary when the provider swaps: (1) the persistence target for typed project state and Phase-3 construction outputs (stable verbs: createProject / readProject / commit / stageArtifact / storeConstructionOutput / retrieveOutputTree), and (2) the provider's collaboration/authorization surface (app installation and tokens, secrets, branch protection, branches, pull requests, reviews, merges). One axis fronted by three ResourceAccess components, one per stable verb family: projectStateAccess (typed state), artifactAccess (construction outputs), sourceControlAccess (collaboration/authorization). The former 'GitHubAppLifecycle' is a mechanism of the current GitHub instance, not an axis of change.

items.name: Work Tracking
items.rationale: At the same moment, different customers track the Method's activities in different work-item systems: GitHub Issues today → Jira / Linear / Azure Boards later. Each Method activity is mirrored as a work item: open-on-seal, status-mirror, close-on-merge. The verbs are stable (openWorkItem / updateWorkItem / closeWorkItem; deferred observeWorkItem); the tracker behind them varies per customer. Independent seam from the repo/CI seams — a customer can run GitHub repo + GitHub Actions + Jira issues, or GitLab + GitLab CI + Linear, with no Manager/Engine-layer change.

items.name: Payment Provider
items.rationale: Which merchant gateway executes aiarch's money movement — charging customers for cycle amounts and validating stored instruments. Current instances: Stripe live, and a deterministic fake in the test profile; regional or alternative gateways (Adyen, Braintree) later, potentially differing per customer geography at the same moment. The verbs are stable (chargeCustomer / validateStoredInstrument); the gateway behind them — its idempotency mechanics and webhook shapes — is what varies. Encapsulated by merchantGatewayAccess. The former 'MerchantGateway' encapsulates-value was the resource noun, not the axis.

items.name: Operational State Store
items.rationale: Which storage substrate holds the platform's own operational records — operated-system state, billing state, and the usage log. Current instances: Postgres in the cloud deployment; in-memory/embedded stores in the test profile; managed alternatives later, potentially per record family. The atomic business verbs on each record family are stable (readOperatedSystem / publishDesiredState / recordRuntimeStatusChange…; readBilling / settleCycle / resettleCycle…; recordComputeUsage / recordFinalUsage / readRange); the substrate behind them — engine, consistency mechanics (version + applied_mutation dedup vs append-dedup), and hosting — varies per deployment profile at one moment and can migrate per record family over time. One axis fronted by three ResourceAccess components, each converting one verb family: operatedSystemStateAccess, billingStateAccess, usageAccess — parallel to Source Control Target being one provider axis fronted by three RAs. Deliberately distinct from Source Control Target: these are transactional row/append stores, not the per-project git repo. The former per-RA encapsulates values were resource nouns and a mechanism, not axes.

decisions.rejectionReason: Question-type review comments (ratified 2026-07-05): a reviewer asks the PM or the architect for clarification without sending the draft back - a permutation of the Drive System Design review gate over the human-collaboration area of volatility. Open questions warn at approve but never gate it; a committed artifact seeds a question-only thread on main. No redraft and no orchestration of its own.

useCase.name: Send Back Change Requests for a Redraft

nodes.label: Reviewer anchors change-request comments to locations in the staged draft
Send the draft back; open change requests now gate approve
Comments persist in the durable review thread on the session branch
Redraft weaves every change request into the next draft
The drafting agent records a per-comment response in the thread
Amended draft re-staged for review
Reviewer re-reviews the new draft against the thread
Change requests resolved?
Close the comments; approve is unblocked

decisions.rejectionReason: Change-request review comments: the reviewer's send-back with anchored, approve-gating comments that drive a redraft - the rejection permutation of the Drive System Design review gate. Feedback is retained in the durable thread on the session branch and each comment is answered by the redraft; same session, same ledger, no business behaviour beyond the core review loop.

components.name: WebClient
components.encapsulates: Transport entry point for the browser SPA — one concrete binding of the Customer Interaction Channel volatility (R-007: more than one entry channel). How the architect user reaches the platform varies per customer and over time without touching any Manager.

components.name: MCPClient
components.encapsulates: Transport entry point for MCP tooling (Claude Code and peer agents) — the second concrete binding of the Customer Interaction Channel volatility (R-007). Same Manager entries as the web client (R4 cross-surface equivalence); only the transport binding differs.

components.name: SchedulerClient
components.encapsulates: Timer-driven entry point for recurring triggers (executeNextActivity, reconcileOperatedState, closeBillingCycle, runShortfallSweep) — binds the Customer Interaction Transport volatility for machine-initiated work. Realized today as Temporal Schedules registered by the operations/billing Manager workers via RegisterSchedule (no standalone binary); the component boundary keeps that realization swappable.

components.name: SystemDesignManager
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the System Design Phase Workflow volatility (R-002, R-003): the Phase-1 artifact co-authoring sequence, the per-artifact human review gate, back-edge amendments with staleness propagation/acknowledgement, and the Phase-1 seal — the rail choreography evolves without disturbing Engines or ResourceAccess.

components.name: ProjectDesignManager
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Project Design Phase Workflow volatility (R-004): how Phase-2 options are drafted, estimated, presented at SDP review, and committed — the option set and review choreography change over time while all estimation math stays in Engines.

components.name: ConstructionManager
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Construction Phase Workflow volatility (R-015, R-016): per-activity execution choreography — hand-off, branch/PR rail, agentic-job dispatch, review routing, variance intervention, pause/override/takeover — which shifts as the construction operating model evolves.

components.name: OperationsManager
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Operation Phase Workflow volatility (R-008, R-023): deploy-after-construction, desired-state reconcile, health intervention, autoscaling application, and delinquency response for operated systems.

components.name: BillingManager
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Billing Workflow volatility (R-013): cycle close, charge, retry/dunning, dispute resettlement, and delinquency escalation — the billing choreography varies as commercial terms evolve while pricing math lives in the Billing Engine.

components.name: EstimationEngine
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Construction Estimation Model volatility (R-004, R-005): project-network/critical-path math, earned-value computation, and per-option duration/cost/risk — the estimation model (e.g. AI-agent token pricing) changes independently of any workflow.

components.name: OperationEstimationEngine
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Operation Estimation Model volatility (R-024, R-025): usage-to-operating-cost curves, current run-rate projection, and what-if scale curves — the cost model varies per infrastructure kind and over time.

components.name: BillingEngine
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Service Pricing volatility (R-013): how a cycle's usage plus commercial terms becomes a charge, and a recomputed charge on dispute/refund correction — pricing policy changes without touching the billing workflow or the payment gateway.

components.name: HandOffEngine
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Construction Hand-Off Policy volatility (R-006): which worker class (architect/senior/junior; human or agent) is picked for an activity — the policy varies across customers and evolves with agent capability.

components.name: InterventionEngine
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Intervention Policy volatility (R-016): the Retry/Escalate/Takeover (and billing Delay) decision rules applied to construction variance, operated-system health changes, and declined charges.

components.name: AutoscalerEngine
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Autoscaling Policy volatility (R-023): how observed telemetry plus policy becomes a desired-state proposal (NoChange/ScaleUp/ScaleDown/Pause/Resume) per infrastructure kind.

components.name: ReviewEngine
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Review Policy volatility (R-003, R-016): which reviewer set a produced change must pass, computed from the architecture graph, frozen contracts, and artifact kind — routing rules change as the quality process matures.

components.name: ProjectStateAccess
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Source Control Target volatility for project state (R-014): atomic business verbs (stage/commit/reject/withdraw artifact, advancePhase, record activity transitions) over the git-as-DB typed aggregate, hiding ref-CAS commit mechanics and the idempotency dedup ledger so the substrate could change without touching Managers.

components.name: OperatedSystemStateAccess
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Operational State Store volatility for operated-system rows (R-008): readOperatedSystem/publishDesiredState/recordRuntimeStatusChange/withdrawSystem hide the guarded in-place update (version + applied-mutation dedup) and the store choice from the Operations workflow.

components.name: BillingStateAccess
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Operational State Store volatility for billing rows (R-013): registerCustomer/bindPaymentInstrument/settleCycle/resettleCycle hide the guarded-update mechanics and the store choice from the Billing workflow.

components.name: WorkItemAccess
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Work Tracking volatility (R-011): openWorkItem/updateWorkItem/closeWorkItem as atomic verbs over whichever tracker the customer uses (GitHub Issues today), so tracker choice never leaks into Managers. buildStatus=planned: no server/internal/resourceaccess/workitem package exists yet.

components.name: ConstructionPipelineAccess
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Construction Pipeline Target volatility (R-015): submit/observe/cancel of agentic-coding jobs, hiding the concrete pipeline (GitHub Actions running claude-code-action on customer-owned compute today) behind pipeline-neutral verbs.

components.name: OperatedRuntimeAccess
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Operated Runtime volatility (R-008, R-026): publishDesiredState/withdraw/getApplicationHealth/getSloStatus/readComputeAttribution over whichever runtime hosts operated systems, keeping runtime choice out of the Operations workflow.

components.name: UsageAccess
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Operational State Store volatility for the usage ledger (R-013, R-024): append and read-range verbs over compute-usage entries, hiding the log substrate from Operations and Billing.

components.name: MerchantGatewayAccess
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Payment Provider volatility (R-013): chargeCustomer/validateStoredInstrument as atomic verbs carrying provider idempotency semantics (Stripe charge-only Connect today), so provider choice never reaches the Billing workflow.

components.name: SourceControlAccess
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Source Control Target volatility for repo operations (R-014, R-015): installation-token, branch, pull-request, review, and merge verbs over the customer-owned repo host (GitHub App today), hiding host API mechanics from Managers.

components.name: DurableExecutionAccess
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Durable Execution Runtime volatility (R-016): registerSchedule/deliverSignal (plus workflow execute/signal/query) as verbs over the durable-execution substrate (Temporal today), so Managers never bind to a runtime API directly.

components.name: ArtifactAccess
components.encapsulates: Encapsulates the Source Control Target volatility for Phase-3 outputs (R-009, R-014): store/retrieve of content-addressed construction outputs in the customer-owned repo, hiding the storage layout from Construction and Operations.

components.name: ProjectGitRepo
components.encapsulates: The customer-owned per-project git repository — the physical store for the typed project state (git-as-DB) and content-addressed Phase-3 construction outputs (R-014: one customer-owned store per scenario, retrievable outright).

components.name: OperatedSystemState
components.encapsulates: Physical store of operated-system rows (desired state, runtime status, deployable-bundle refs), mutated only via guarded in-place updates through OperatedSystemStateAccess (R-008).

components.name: BillingState
components.encapsulates: Physical store of billing rows (customers, payment-instrument bindings, cycles, settlements), reached only through BillingStateAccess (R-013).

components.name: ConstructionPipelineRuntime
components.encapsulates: The external pipeline execution fabric that runs agentic-coding jobs — GitHub Actions running claude-code-action on customer-owned compute (R-015); reached only through ConstructionPipelineAccess.

components.name: OperatedRuntime
components.encapsulates: The runtime hosting deployed customer systems, exposing health/SLO/compute-attribution telemetry (R-008, R-026); reached only through OperatedRuntimeAccess.

components.name: UsageLog
components.encapsulates: Append-only physical ledger of compute-usage entries feeding cost projection and usage-based billing (R-013, R-024); reached only through UsageAccess.

components.name: DurableExecutionRuntime
components.encapsulates: The durable-execution substrate (Temporal) hosting Manager workflows, schedules, and signals. Deliberate exemption per the operational-concepts decision 'durable primitives are execution substrate, not architecture edges': a Manager's workflow-internal durable primitives (timers, awaitSignal, child workflows) do NOT appear as edges — only explicit cross-component verbs (registerSchedule, deliverSignal) surface, via DurableExecutionAccess.

components.name: MerchantGateway
components.encapsulates: The external payment provider (Stripe, charge-only Connect) executing charges/refunds against the customer's stored instrument (R-013); reached only through MerchantGatewayAccess.

components.name: GitHub
components.encapsulates: The external repo host carrying the GitHub App installation, per-project repos, branches, pull requests, and Actions triggers (R-014, R-015); reached only through SourceControlAccess.

components.name: WorkItemTracker
components.encapsulates: The external work-tracking system (GitHub Issues today) holding per-activity work items (R-011). buildStatus=planned — EARMARK: the UC2/UC3 call chains traverse WorkItemAccess → WorkItemTracker but no server/internal/resourceaccess/workitem package exists yet; decide at the next redraft whether to build the RA or re-route the work-item steps.

components.name: Security
components.encapsulates: Utility: authentication/authorization for every entry channel (Keycloak-backed AuthN/AuthZ; service identity for timer triggers). Passes the cappuccino-machine test — serves any system, owns no business volatility.

components.name: Logging
components.encapsulates: Utility: structured logging sink every Manager reports to. Passes the cappuccino-machine test — serves any system, owns no business volatility.

components.name: Diagnostics
components.encapsulates: Utility: health and telemetry reporting surface (OTel) for every Manager. Passes the cappuccino-machine test — serves any system, owns no business volatility.

decisions.decision: External callers reach in-process Clients through Envoy Gateway; Clients call exactly one Manager per request via Temporal (durableExecutionAccess.startOrSignalWorkflow). No internal message bus; no synchronous HTTP between Managers. A durable-execution runtime is mandatory for every Manager without exception.

Closed layering per App C §3.4. No Client-to-Engine, Client-to-ResourceAccess, or skip-layer edges. One permitted exception: the queued BillingManager-to-OperationsManager applyDelinquencyPolicy Temporal Signal (queued M→M per §3.4c.ii).

Every LLM-needing activity (Phase-1 drafting, Phase-2 plan drafting, Phase-3 construction) is dispatched as an agentic-coding job — a GitHub Action running claude-code-action in the user's own GitHub repo on the user's own CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN and CI minutes. aiarch holds no LLM key, makes no synchronous LLM call, and holds no code text; it orchestrates via constructionPipelineAccess and reads results back from git via projectStateAccess.

decisions.topic: artifact presentation is a client concern
decisions.decision: The server does not render Method artifacts. It exposes typed Method models as JSON; the React SPA renders them client-side (React-Flow C4 graph, two-axis SVG scatter, React-Flow activity diagrams, react-markdown prose). renderingEngine and renderingToolchain are removed. Every typed model becomes part of the frozen wire contract via the OpenAPI discriminated modelEnvelope.

decisions.topic: catalog read — getProject single typed read
decisions.decision: projectManager.getProject(projectId) returns the full typed project head-state in one synchronous read (phase, version, research, every artifact slot with kind/stage/model/findings/critique). Thin pass-through over projectStateAccess.ReadProject; no per-artifact fan-out, no durable workflow. Powers both the home-base TOC and the design-experience render path.

decisions.topic: project state storage — git JSON with ref CAS
decisions.decision: Project state is JSON files in the per-project git repo (behind projectStateAccess), mutated by guarded commits under git ref compare-and-swap (push rejected non-fast-forward → reload + retry). Activity-retry idempotency is an in-repo applied_mutation record keyed on workflowId:activityId. The separate project-state Postgres cluster is removed. Operated-system and billing head-state remain Postgres aggregates with version-guarded UPDATE + applied_mutation dedup.

Every aiarch service is written in Go (latest stable). The unsafe package is forbidden in aiarch source via depguard; transitive unsafe use is audited via SBOM + govulncheck. go test -race runs on every CI test run. Third-party dependencies only when ubiquitous AND absolutely necessary (launch set: go.temporal.io/sdk, github.com/jackc/pgx/v5).

decisions.topic: generated REST clients — Go + TypeScript from one OpenAPI spec
decisions.decision: Every Manager owns one OpenAPI spec. Codegen produces a Go package and a TypeScript npm package per Manager from that spec. The MCP tool surface is a further mechanical projection of the same per-Manager OpenAPI specs (each operation becomes one MCP tool). Both codegen outputs are committed and drift is detected in CI.

decisions.topic: billing model — usage-based cloud hosting only
decisions.decision: aiarch bills the user a usage-based hosting fee for operated systems (DEPLOYED — OPERATED only); there is no flat subscription and construction is not charged (construction runs on the user's own Claude Code account and GitHub Actions). billingEngine pivots on ServicePricing as a Strategy (tier floors, free-tier subsidies; no token unit price). aiarch is merchant of record only for its own service invoices; it never touches end-user revenue.

decisions.topic: deployment scenarios are configuration, not architecture
decisions.decision: Two production scenarios (DEPLOYED — NOT OPERATED; DEPLOYED — OPERATED) differ only by an operation on/off flag — OperationsManager and OperatedRuntime and hosting billing are not instantiated in the not-operated scenario. A test profile swaps the durable-execution runtime to the embedded executor, the git target to an ephemeral throwaway repo, and the agentic-coding CI to a local exec runner or stub. The component graph, call chains, and component count are identical across all scenarios.

decisions.topic: durable primitives are execution substrate, not architecture edges
decisions.decision: A Manager's workflow-internal durable primitives (startTimer, awaitSignal, executeChild) and each Manager's own client.Client are EXECUTION SUBSTRATE, not architecture edges — they are how a Manager's own workflow runs on the durable runtime, not a call from one component to another. The systemDesign durable-execution-access edges therefore depict CLIENT-SIDE verb use only (deliverSignal / registerSchedule against another Manager's workflow), never a Manager's timers/awaits/child-workflows over its own encapsulated workflow.

reviewThread.text: Reviewed — unaffected: Slot-5 system amendment (rev 2) reviewed against this artifact: call chains added for every nonCore variation, duplicate source-control edges consolidated, encapsulates prose filled on all components. No basis change affects this artifact — unaffected.

items.guideline: Never design against requirements; design against volatility.
Avoid functional decomposition — no container or component named after a feature.
Decompose based on volatility — every component cites a volatilities entry.
items.justification: Verified 2026-07-03: every Manager, Engine, and ResourceAccess carries an encapsulates citation into the 22-item volatilities slot; the three Client-tier and cross-cutting volatilities (Customer Interaction Channel, Customer Interaction Transport, Customer App Infrastructure) name their encapsulators in the volatility rationale.

items.guideline: Composable design — non-core use cases trace through existing components without modification.
Features as integration, not implementation — no Manager named for a feature.
Design iteratively, build incrementally.
items.justification: N/A at Phase 1; not applicable to system design artifacts.

items.guideline: Design the project to build the system.
items.justification: N/A at Phase 1; covered in Phase 2 project design.

items.guideline: Educated decisions with options.
items.justification: N/A at Phase 1; addressed in Phase 2 SDP review.

items.guideline: Build along the critical path.
items.justification: N/A at Phase 1; addressed in Phase 3 construction.

items.guideline: Be on time throughout.
items.justification: N/A at Phase 1; addressed in Phase 3 tracking.

items.guideline: Capture required behavior, not functionality — requirements state what, not how.
Describe required behavior with use cases — actor, trigger, outcome, success path, alternatives.
Document use cases with nested conditions via activity diagrams.
Eliminate solutions masquerading as requirements — scrubbed requirements complete.
Validate by supporting all core use cases — every core UC has a dynamic view in the system model.
items.justification: 5 core use cases, 5 dynamic views (uc1-drive-system-design, uc2-commit-project-option, uc3-execute-construction-activity, uc4-operate-delivered-system, uc5-bill-user-for-usage); each dynamic view enters exactly one Manager.

items.guideline: Rarely more than 6 core use cases.
items.justification: 5 core use cases as of 2026-07-03 (verified in the coreUseCases slot: 5 core, 10 nonCore with rejection reasons). Comfortably within bound.

items.guideline: 5 or fewer Managers without subsystems.
items.justification: 5 Managers as of 2026-07-03: systemDesignManager, projectDesignManager, constructionManager, operationsManager, billingManager. The 2026-06-01 waiver for a 6th thin projectManager is RETIRED — projectManager was removed in the charge-only reconciliation (catalog reads re-homed to systemDesignManager.getProject). Item returns to a clean pass.

items.guideline: Few subsystems.
3 or fewer Managers per subsystem.
items.justification: N/A — zero subsystems in use.

items.guideline: Golden Engines-to-Managers ratio — fewer Engines than Managers (the book's expectation).
items.justification: WAIVED 2026-07-03 (architect disposition): 7 Engines against 5 Managers inverts the book's ratio (5 Managers expect ~3 Engines per ch. 3 cardinality). Consolidation candidates examined and rejected: (1) EstimationEngine + OperationEstimationEngine encapsulate two independently-evolving volatilities (Construction Estimation Model vs Operation Estimation Model) with disjoint caller sets beyond the shared projectDesignManager — merging violates one-volatility-per-encapsulator; (2) InterventionEngine + AutoscalerEngine encapsulate policies on OPPOSITE volatility axes (Intervention Policy = same-customer-over-time; Autoscaling Policy = all-customers-at-one-time) and have different reuse profiles (Intervention shared by 3 Managers, Autoscaler by 1) — merging couples the two axes, the exact smell ch. 2 warns against. Consolidating mid-construction is additionally a scope change cascading through serviceContracts, the activity network, and Go packages. Each of the 7 Engines has a 1:1 volatility-to-encapsulator relationship. Re-evaluate at the next scope-change replan, or immediately if either pair's volatilities begin changing together.

items.guideline: ResourceAccess may serve multiple Resources.
items.justification: Permitted, not required — as of 2026-07-03 each of the 11 ResourceAccess components fronts exactly one Resource; projectGitRepo is fronted by two RAs (projectStateAccess, artifactAccess), which is the converse arrangement and equally legal.

items.guideline: ResourceAccess count within order-of-magnitude bound (~10).
items.justification: 11 production ResourceAccess components as of 2026-07-03: projectStateAccess, artifactAccess, operatedSystemStateAccess, billingStateAccess, constructionPipelineAccess, operatedRuntimeAccess, usageAccess, merchantGatewayAccess (renamed from billingGatewayAccess in the charge-only reconciliation), sourceControlAccess, durableExecutionAccess, workItemAccess. artifactAccess is re-established as its own RA (staging/retrieval of construction outputs), reversing the earlier fold into projectStateAccess C2. Inside the order-of-magnitude bound.

items.guideline: Resource count within order-of-magnitude bound (~10).
items.justification: 10 Resources as of 2026-07-03: 7 internal (projectGitRepo, operatedSystemState, billingState, constructionPipelineRuntime, durableExecutionRuntime, operatedRuntime, usageLog) + 3 external systems (merchantGateway, github, workItemTracker). Inside bound.

items.guideline: Volatility count within 6–15 target range.
items.justification: WAIVED (re-affirmed 2026-07-03): 22 volatilities in the committed slot — 11 same-customer-over-time, 11 all-customers-at-one-time — over the 6–15 upper bound. Growth from 18 came with the charge-only reconciliation ratified 2026-07-03 (Payment Provider, interaction channel/transport split, operational-state entries). Every entry has exactly one named encapsulator and a business trace; no speculative subdivision. Compression candidates (Construction Estimation Model + Operation Estimation Model; Intervention Policy + Autoscaling Policy) rejected for the same axis-independence reasons recorded under §2d. Re-evaluate if the count grows past 25 or any entry loses its 1:1 encapsulator.

items.guideline: Volatility decreases top-down through the layers.
Reuse increases top-down — Utilities most reusable, Managers single-purpose.
Do not encapsulate nature-of-business changes.
Managers should be almost expendable — all Engines, RA, Resources, and Utilities survive a Manager rewrite.
Symmetric design — all Managers receive direct Client calls, use Strategy-style Engines, read/write through ResourceAccess, log via Utilities.
items.justification: All 5 Managers receive exactly one Client entry per use case and call at least one Engine (verified 2026-07-03: systemDesignManager to estimationEngine; projectDesignManager to estimation/operationEstimation/billing; constructionManager to handOff/review/intervention; operationsManager to autoscaler/operationEstimation/intervention; billingManager to billing/intervention). The single queued M-to-M edge is the only asymmetry and is documented in operational concepts.

items.guideline: No public channels for internal interactions — no internal pub/sub.
Resources serve one purpose — each Resource is fronted by ResourceAccess encapsulating a distinct volatility.
items.justification: As of 2026-07-03 projectGitRepo is fronted by two RAs with distinct access volatilities: projectStateAccess (typed head-state verbs under git ref CAS) and artifactAccess (construction-output staging/retrieval). This reverses the 2026-06-15 fold-in: the two write-disciplines proved to be separate volatilities, so each got its own access component. Every other Resource is fronted by exactly one RA.

items.guideline: Avoid open architecture — closed layer model declared.
Avoid semi-closed/semi-open architecture.
Prefer closed layering.
Do not call up — every edge goes within a layer or down to a lower layer.
No sideways edges except queued M-to-M or M-to-E.
items.justification: Exactly one sideways edge exists as of 2026-07-03 and it is the permitted form: billingManager queues applyDelinquencyPolicy to operationsManager (mode=queued). All 12 Manager-to-Engine edges are sync in-process calls. No other sideways edges.

items.guideline: No skipping layers — Clients reach Managers directly; Clients do not reach Engine/RA/Resource.
Resolve open architecture attempts via queued or async calls.
items.justification: The one cross-Manager flow (billing delinquency escalation into operations) is resolved exactly this way — a queued call, documented in the operational-concepts layering-style decision. No unresolved open attempts.

items.guideline: Extend the system by implementing subsystems.
items.justification: N/A — forward-looking; no subsystems needed at current Manager count.

items.guideline: All components can call Utilities — every Client and Manager calls Security/Logging/Diagnostics.
Managers and Engines can call ResourceAccess; Engines must not do I/O.
items.justification: All 7 Engines (estimation, operationEstimation, handOff, intervention, autoscaler, review, billing) are pure-computation functions of their inputs; no Engine holds a ResourceAccess dependency or does I/O.

items.guideline: Managers can call Engines — every Manager calls at least one Engine.
items.justification: Verified 2026-07-03 against the relationships slot: all 5 Managers have at least one sync Engine edge (systemDesignManager has exactly one, to estimationEngine).

items.guideline: Managers can queue calls to another Manager.
items.justification: Used exactly once as of 2026-07-03: billingManager queues applyDelinquencyPolicy to operationsManager (the delinquency suspend/resume escalation). All other cross-Manager transitions remain Client-initiated. No internal bus.

items.guideline: Clients do not call multiple Managers per use case.
items.justification: Verified 2026-07-03: each of the 5 dynamic views enters exactly one Manager from its Client.

items.guideline: Managers do not queue to more than one Manager per use case.
items.justification: billingManager is the only queueing Manager and queues to exactly one Manager (operationsManager) in the billing use case. No Manager queues to two.

items.guideline: Engines do not receive queued calls.
items.justification: All 12 Manager-to-Engine edges are mode=sync; the sole queued edge in the system is Manager-to-Manager.

items.guideline: ResourceAccess does not receive queued calls.
items.justification: All RA inbound edges are sync in-process calls; the sole queued edge in the system is Manager-to-Manager.

items.guideline: Clients do not publish events.
items.justification: Trivially satisfied — no bus. Each Client makes a direct in-process call to exactly one Manager method per inbound request. The prior Don't 6e waiver (Clients publishing onto the bus) remains revoked.

items.guideline: Engines do not publish events.
items.justification: Trivially satisfied.

items.guideline: ResourceAccess does not publish events.
items.justification: Trivially satisfied.

items.guideline: Resources do not publish events.
items.justification: Trivially satisfied.

items.guideline: Engines, ResourceAccess, and Resources do not subscribe to events.
items.justification: Trivially satisfied.

items.guideline: Engine names are gerunds (ch. 3 naming rule: gerund + Engine suffix, e.g. MatchingEngine).
items.justification: WAIVED 2026-07-03 (architect disposition): 6 of 7 Engines are noun-named rather than gerund-named (EstimationEngine, OperationEstimationEngine, HandOffEngine, InterventionEngine, AutoscalerEngine, ReviewEngine; only BillingEngine conforms). The rule is a readability convention, not a structural one — each Engine's classification, purity, and volatility encapsulation are correct. Renaming mid-construction would cascade through the systemDesign component ids, serviceContracts keys, the activity network, and Go package/component names while construction agents are actively working in server/ — cost far exceeds the convention's benefit now. Re-evaluation trigger: apply gerund names opportunistically whenever an architectural change next touches a given Engine's contract, and at the latest during the next major-version decomposition review.

items.guideline: Temporal vocabulary on architecture edges (skill Section I, 7a–7j).
items.justification: Marked N/A by design: the operational-concepts decision 'durable primitives are execution substrate, not architecture edges' deliberately keeps Temporal primitives (startTimer, awaitSignal, executeChild, client.Client) off the typed relationship labels — edges carry business verbs with a sync/queued mode instead. Determinism and idempotency discipline live in operational concepts and per-component contracts, not in edge labels.

items.guideline: Service contracts: per-component op-count within sweet spot (3–5 typical, 12 hard cap); 2 or fewer contracts per ResourceAccess.
items.justification: Forward-look enforced per component at detailed design. Structure as of 2026-07-03: the former projectStateAccess C2 (construction-output staging/retrieval) is now the separate artifactAccess component, so no RA carries more than 2 contracts; sourceControlAccess retains its repo-lifecycle + pull-request-rail split. Full App B walk re-runs per contract during Phase 3 detailed-design activities.

items.guideline: Identical component set in test and production — no test-only production seams.
items.justification: workerAccess removed entirely (charge-only architecture: no direct LLM access component — construction runs on the user's own Claude Code account via agentic GitHub jobs). The LLM is stubbed at the agentic-job boundary (constructionPipelineAccess/sourceControlAccess), not at an internal seam. Internal component set is genuinely identical in test and prod.

items.guideline: Mission integrity and traceability — every volatility has an encapsulator; every objective has a component.
items.justification: Verified 2026-07-03 across all 22 volatilities: 19 cited directly by component encapsulates fields; Customer Interaction Channel and Customer Interaction Transport encapsulated by the Client tier; Customer App Infrastructure encapsulated via Strategy registrations on AutoscalerEngine and OperationEstimationEngine (recorded in the volatility rationale).

items.guideline: Structurizr DSL validity — the rendered architecture DSL validates clean.
items.justification: The DSL is render-on-read of the typed systemDesign slot; the typed model (39 components, 5 dynamic views) is the source of truth and round-trips through the Go codec.

items.guideline: Billing model: usage-based cloud hosting charge only; no flat subscription; construction runs on the user's own Claude Code account; aiarch is a pure service vendor.
items.justification: Charge-only architecture ratified 2026-07-03: BillingManager + BillingEngine + MerchantGatewayAccess (Stripe Connect direct charges — payment provider is the encapsulated Payment Provider volatility; aiarch is NOT merchant of record for customer apps) + UsageAccess implement the one-directional hosting charge (meter hosting/compute, price via ServicePricing, charge the user). No flat subscription; no token metering; no construction charges; no revenue-share. Delinquency escalates via the queued billingManager-to-operationsManager edge.

model.notes: Team: solo founder (bus-factor-1) + up to 3 concurrent Claude agent streams (architect/PM/product-manager/specialist roles). Founder works ~2 effective days/week (40% of nominal). Parallelism cap: 3 in-flight agent streams; beyond that, founder review quality degrades. Infrastructure: Go + Temporal + Postgres (CNPG for Temporal + billing head-state only; project state is JSON-in-git with git-ref CAS). Construction CI is the user's own GitHub Actions running claude-code-action — aiarch holds no LLM key and does not charge for construction. Billing model (2026-06-09 MoR-reversal): pure one-directional vendor charge — aiarch bills the user a usage-based HOSTING fee only (DEPLOYED — OPERATED scenario); no flat subscription; no revenue share; no charge for construction or LLM work; revenueShare=0 (no payout; the old merchant-of-record / revenue-share model was reversed). computeCost=2 (tieredFloors: tier floors + free-tier subsidies per ServicePricing strategy); schedule=1 (monthly billing period). Risk flags: bus-factor-1; founder availability variable (2d/wk); operated runtime unchosen; durable execution platform unchosen; git-as-project-state-store novelty; AI-agent construction quality variance; dog-food chicken-and-egg. No hard external deadlines. Internal-only v1 audience.

scenarios.title: Drive System Design (client op: driveDesignPhase)
scenarios.description: Black-box exercise of the Phase-1 design loop at the systemDesignManager surface. Proves the artifact draft->review->commit state machine and the phase-advance gate; breaks it with premature advance, a reject-redraft loop, and an out-of-order draft request.

cases.title: Full artifact loop: create -> research -> draft mission -> approve -> commit; advance once Phase-1 complete
cases.proves: The design loop drives an artifact from draft to committed and the phase gate opens only when every Phase-1 slot is committed (Method-conformance objective #2).
cases.expectedOutcome: Mission reaches StageCommitted; with all Phase-1 kinds 0-7 committed, AdvancePhase returns advanced=true, missingArtifacts=[].

steps.assertion: Returns a non-empty ProjectID for the new project.

inputs.name: research
inputs.value: {"sources":[{"title":"Founder brief","content":"A platform that engineers software predictably and hosts it for a usage fee."}]}

steps.assertion: Returns an incremented Version (>=1); research is persisted before drafting begins.
Returns a non-empty SessionRef; the Phase-1 workflow is live.
kind=0 (KindMission); returns a SessionRef and enqueues the architect-draft job.
Once the draft lands, stage=3 (StageAwaitingReview) and draft.kind="mission".
decision=1 (ReviewApprove); no error. A follow-up GetSessionState shows stage=5 (StageCommitted).
PRECONDITION: kinds 0-7 all committed. advanced=true and missingArtifacts is empty - the phase seals. acknowledgeStale=false — no stale basis is in play in this scenario.

cases.title: AdvancePhase refused before all Phase-1 artifacts are committed
cases.proves: The phase gate refuses to advance a half-designed system - the core precondition failure of Method conformance.
cases.expectedOutcome: AdvancePhase returns advanced=false with missingArtifacts enumerating every uncommitted Phase-1 kind; no phase transition occurs.

steps.assertion: Only the mission (kind=0) is committed; glossary...standardCheck remain uncommitted.

advanced=false; missingArtifacts=[Glossary,ScrubbedRequirements,Volatilities,CoreUseCases,System,OperationalConcepts,StandardCheck]. The system must NOT advance. acknowledgeStale=false — no stale basis is in play in this scenario.

cases.title: Reject decision drives a redraft, never a silent commit
cases.proves: A ReviewReject must not commit the artifact and must re-open drafting - exposing any code path that treats reject as a no-op or (worse) commits anyway.
cases.expectedOutcome: After reject, stage=4 (StageRedrafting), NOT StageCommitted; a fresh draft session is spawned carrying the feedback.

steps.assertion: stage=3 (StageAwaitingReview) - a draft is pending review.

inputs.name: feedback
inputs.value: {"notes":"Mission conflates vision with objectives; separate them.","comments":null}

steps.assertion: decision=2 (ReviewReject) with substantive feedback; call returns without error.
stage=4 (StageRedrafting), NOT 5 (StageCommitted). The mission slot remains uncommitted.

cases.title: Draft requested for an artifact whose predecessor slots are uncommitted
cases.proves: The data-dependency ordering is enforced: you cannot draft core use cases (kind=4) before volatilities (kind=3) are committed. Exposes any path that lets the design skip its own dependency graph.
cases.expectedOutcome: RequestArtifactDraft is refused with a precondition/illegal-state error; no session is created.

steps.assertion: kind=4 (KindCoreUseCases) requested while kind=3 (KindVolatilities) is uncommitted -> refused. No SessionRef returned.

scenarios.title: Commit to a Project Option (client op: driveDesignPhase / SDP)
scenarios.description: Black-box exercise of the projectDesignManager SDP-commit surface. Proves that committing an option opens work items and advances to construction; breaks it with premature AdvanceToConstruction, a reject-all decision, and an unknown option id.

cases.title: Commit the normal option, then advance to construction
cases.proves: A valid SDPCommit records the chosen option and lets construction open (mirrors uc2 chain: SubmitSDPDecision -> commitOption -> openWorkItem).
cases.expectedOutcome: SubmitSDPDecision(commit, "normal") succeeds; AdvanceToConstruction returns advanced=true, missingArtifacts=[].

steps.assertion: Returns a SessionRef; the SDP review is assembled from all four options.
kind=16 (KindSdpReview); stage=3 (StageAwaitingReview), draft.kind="sdpReview".
decision=1 (SDPCommit) with a real option id; no error. chosenOption is recorded and work items open.
advanced=true; the project enters Phase 3. acknowledgeStale=false — no stale basis is in play in this scenario.

cases.title: AdvanceToConstruction before any option is committed
cases.proves: The construction gate refuses to open until an option is committed - the canonical 'advance before all slots committed' precondition violation.
cases.expectedOutcome: AdvanceToConstruction returns advanced=false with missingArtifacts including SdpReview (16); the project stays in Phase 2.

steps.assertion: SDP is assembled but NOT yet decided.
advanced=false; missingArtifacts contains 16 (KindSdpReview). No work items opened. acknowledgeStale=false — no stale basis is in play in this scenario.

cases.title: SDPRejectAll keeps the project in design and blocks construction
cases.proves: Rejecting all options must not silently pick one; the project must remain re-plannable and construction must stay closed.
cases.expectedOutcome: After SDPRejectAll, no chosenOption is recorded; a follow-up AdvanceToConstruction still returns advanced=false.

inputs.name: feedback
inputs.value: {"notes":"All four options exceed budget; re-plan at lower staffing.","comments":null}

steps.assertion: decision=2 (SDPRejectAll); accepted with feedback, no option committed.
advanced=false - reject-all does not open construction. acknowledgeStale=false — no stale basis is in play in this scenario.

cases.title: SDPCommit references an option id that is not in the SDP review — refused asynchronously by the workflow
cases.proves: Option-set validation happens INSIDE the SDP-review workflow, not at the Manager edge: SubmitSDPDecision is a fire-and-forget Temporal signal, so a garbage/typo optionID is ACCEPTED at the surface but must never commit an option. Exposes any path that commits a garbage option — and any client that mis-models the async refusal as a synchronous error.
cases.expectedOutcome: SubmitSDPDecision(commit, unknown option) is accepted (signal enqueued, no error). The SDP workflow terminates with UnknownOption before any commit transition: the session-state read still shows stage=3 (StageAwaitingReview, never StageCommitted), no chosenOption is recorded, and AdvanceToConstruction still gates with missingArtifacts=[16].

steps.assertion: The SDP session is live and the option set is assembled (stage reaches AwaitingReview) — the signal in the next step has a running workflow to land on.

decision=1 (SDPCommit) with an unknown optionID is ACCEPTED — the Manager validates shape only (non-empty optionID) and enqueues the sdpDecision signal; option-set membership is validated asynchronously inside the workflow.

The async validation refused the commit: the workflow terminated with UnknownOption BEFORE any stage transition, so the session-state read (poll until the signal is consumed) still shows stage=3 (StageAwaitingReview) — never StageCommitted. No chosenOption recorded.

The construction gate is still closed: the garbage option never committed the SdpReview slot (16), so advanced=false with missingArtifacts=[16]. acknowledgeStale=false — no stale basis is in play in this scenario.

scenarios.title: Execute a Construction Activity (client op: superviseConstruction)
scenarios.description: Black-box exercise of the constructionManager pump + review-gate surface. Proves that ExecuteNextActivity respects the dependency network, that send-back blocks a merge, and that the pump is tick-idempotent (retries do not double-dispatch).

cases.title: Pump dispatches the next eligible activity and drives it through the review gate
cases.proves: ExecuteNextActivity picks the next eligible activity, and a PhaseApprove advances it toward merge/exit (mirrors uc3 chain: executeNextActivity -> PickWorkerClass -> ... -> ProposeReviews -> mergePullRequest -> recordActivityExited).
cases.expectedOutcome: First tick dispatches an activity (dispatched=true); PhaseApprove is accepted; a later tick progresses the activity to exit.

steps.assertion: dispatched=true and activityId is non-null - the first eligible activity (no unmet predecessors) is dispatched.
Session view shows the activity in-progress with a branch/PR opened.
decision=1 (PhaseApprove); no error. The activity advances to the construction phase.
A later tick progresses construction; on approved review the PR merges and the activity records an exit.

cases.title: Pump does not dispatch when no activity is eligible
cases.proves: The pump honors the dependency network: with all remaining activities blocked by unmet predecessors, it must NOT dispatch out of order.
cases.expectedOutcome: ExecuteNextActivity returns dispatched=false, activityId=null; no branch/PR is opened.

steps.assertion: dispatched=false - quiescent when the frontier is empty; no out-of-order construction, no wasted pipeline.

cases.title: PhaseSendBack blocks the merge and re-opens the phase
cases.proves: A send-back review verdict must not merge the PR or exit the activity - exposing any path that merges despite a rejecting reviewer.
cases.expectedOutcome: After PhaseSendBack, the activity reverts to redraft; the PR is not merged and the activity is not recorded as exited.

steps.assertion: decision=2 (PhaseSendBack) accepted with feedback.
Session shows the phase re-opened for redraft; PR NOT merged, activity NOT exited.

cases.title: Duplicate ExecuteNextActivity with the same tickID is idempotent
cases.proves: Scheduler retries must not double-dispatch: the same tickID must not open a second branch/PR or spend LLM tokens twice (RA-idempotency promoted to the client surface).
cases.expectedOutcome: The second call with the identical tickID does not produce a second dispatch of the same activity (dispatched=false, or the same activityId with no new branch/PR).

steps.assertion: First call dispatches C-BILLENG and opens exactly one branch/PR.
Same tickID replayed -> no second dispatch of C-BILLENG; the branch/PR count for the activity is unchanged.

scenarios.title: Operate a Delivered System (client ops: operateDeliveredSystem / readOperatedSystemView)
scenarios.description: Black-box exercise of the operationsManager surface. Proves deploy->reconcile->observe reaches Healthy; breaks it with the delinquency pause path, deploy idempotency on a duplicate changeId, and terminal withdrawal.

cases.title: Deploy after construction, reconcile, and observe Healthy
cases.proves: A full-bundle deploy publishes desired state and the reconcile loop drives the app to Healthy (mirrors uc4: deployAfterConstruction -> publishDesiredState -> reconcileOperatedState -> getApplicationHealth).
cases.expectedOutcome: DeployResult.published=true; ReconcileResult shows a Pending->Healthy transition; QueryOperatedSystemView reports Phase=RuntimeStatusHealthy.

steps.assertion: reason=1 (ReasonDeployAfterConstruction), patchKind=1 (PatchFullBundle); published=true with a non-null revision.
observed>=1 and transitions>=1 - a status transition (Pending->Healthy) is recorded on change only.
readOperatedSystemView surface: Phase=2 (RuntimeStatusHealthy), InFlight=false, CurrentRunRate present.

cases.title: Delinquency policy pauses (not withdraws) the operated system
cases.proves: The queued Manager->Manager delinquency signal must PAUSE the system when pauseNotWithdraw=true and must be observable - never silently withdraw or ignore it.
cases.expectedOutcome: After ApplyDelinquencyPolicy, publishDesiredState fires with reason=ReasonDelinquency and the view is NOT RuntimeStatusWithdrawn.

steps.assertion: pauseNotWithdraw=true; the manager re-publishes desired state with reason=4 (ReasonDelinquency).
Phase is paused (e.g. 1 RuntimeStatusPending / degraded), and specifically NOT 4 (RuntimeStatusWithdrawn) - pause is reversible when payment clears.

cases.title: Duplicate DeployAfterConstruction with the same changeId is idempotent
cases.proves: Deterministic manifest paths mean a replayed changeId must not create a divergent desired-state revision (RA idempotency asserted black-box at the client surface).
cases.expectedOutcome: Both calls return published=true with the SAME revision; the runtime is not published to twice.

steps.assertion: First deploy with changeId=chg-deploy-001 yields revision rev-1.
Replayed changeId returns the SAME revision (rev-1) - no second revision, no divergent desired state.

cases.title: Withdrawal is terminal; reconcile does not resurrect a withdrawn app
cases.proves: After WithdrawSystem the app must reach RuntimeStatusWithdrawn and the reconcile loop must not republish it - exposing any resurrection bug.
cases.expectedOutcome: WithdrawResult.withdrawn=true; the view Phase=RuntimeStatusWithdrawn; a scoped reconcile republishes 0.

steps.assertion: withdrawn=true; desired state is torn down.
republished=0 - a withdrawn app is not resurrected by reconcile; QueryOperatedSystemView shows Phase=4 (RuntimeStatusWithdrawn).

scenarios.title: Bill the User for Usage (client op: closeBillingCycle)
scenarios.description: Black-box exercise of the billingManager close-cycle surface (the constructed contract: CloseBillingCycle / RunShortfallSweep). Proves a cycle closes into exactly one computed signed net routed exactly once through the merchant gateway; breaks it with a duplicate close (idempotency / no double money move) and a gateway decline (shortfall sweep path, no phantom billing). Step inputs are grounded in the uc5 dynamic-view signatures (closeBillingCycle(customerId, cycleId), etc.). NOTE: billing is deferred build scope — the backing RAs (billingState, revenueLedger, merchantGateway) are generated not-implemented stubs on main; this scenario activates with that scope.

cases.title: Close a billing cycle -> compute net -> route money once
cases.proves: Closing a cycle reads revenue + usage, computes the signed net, routes it exactly once, and settles the cycle (mirrors uc5: closeBillingCycle -> readBilling -> readRange x2 -> ComputeNet -> charge|payout -> settleCycle).
cases.expectedOutcome: Exactly one settled cycle is recorded for (customer, cycle); the gateway moves money once under Idempotency-Key settle:cust-88:2026-06.

steps.assertion: One settled cycle is recorded for cust-88/2026-06; usageAccess.ReadRange, revenueLedgerAccess.ReadRange, billingEngine.ComputeNet, and exactly one merchantGatewayAccess.ChargeCustomer OR PayoutCustomer occur; no error.

cases.title: Duplicate closeBillingCycle for the same cycle does not move money twice
cases.proves: The load-bearing money-safety invariant: replaying closeBillingCycle for the same (customer, cycle) must not produce a second billing or a second gateway money move (same Stripe Idempotency-Key; settleCycle applied_mutation dedup).
cases.expectedOutcome: The second close is a no-op with respect to money: settled-cycle count stays 1 and the gateway is not invoked a second time.

steps.assertion: First close settles the cycle and moves money once.
Replay for the same cycle: still exactly one billing; the gateway is NOT re-invoked (Idempotency-Key settle:cust-88:2026-06 dedupes). No double move.

cases.title: Gateway decline leaves the cycle unsettled and drives the shortfall sweep - no phantom billing
cases.proves: A declined shortfall charge must surface as a failure and leave the cycle open (feeding DecideOnBillingFailure / the delinquency path), never record a billing that did not collect.
cases.expectedOutcome: chargeCustomer declines; the cycle is not marked settled; runShortfallSweep re-attempts the open shortfall without duplicating it.

steps.assertion: merchantGatewayAccess.ChargeCustomer returns card_declined; the cycle stays unsettled, no billing is recorded; interventionEngine.DecideOnBillingFailure chooses Retry | Escalate | Delay.
The sweep re-attempts the single open shortfall for cust-declined-99; it does not create a second billing and it feeds ApplyDelinquencyPolicy if it keeps failing.
